text:
Meeting Title: Kam x Careerspan Founders Call

Calendar Event ID: 35180e65-6ce1-4f95-b45b-9b7e40c6d349

Attendee Emails: 

Creator Email: vrijen@mycareerspan.com

Transcript:

 
Me: I had WhatsApp clothes. Sorry. Great conversation.  
Them: Good guy. I liked him. He had, like, a chill vibe.  
Me: Good. Yeah. They're operating at a caliber way above us, but I think in terms of PMF signs, that was good.  
Them: Yeah, I think. You might be right about the community. The clothing, the doors. Community only access. From a signal to employers and recruiters perspective.  
Me: Even if we did just do it for a bit again, we're hamstrung on resources. If we do it for a bit, I think we. As a startup, you're allowed a certain amount of total rebrands, right?  
Them: I would want to. Yeah.  
Me: I don't think we should do a total rebrand, but to the extent that this is a total rebrand, it's a total rebrand. But I think we get. I think we did one from apply to Careerspan. I think we're due one. And I think we can make one where we make a big fuss and say we're pulling from these communities, we're going to expand access, but this is where we're starting. We can sort of figure out the messaging and not go completely craven about this, But I do think that limiting it to those communities will make it both more appealing for the communities and more appealing for the folks that were scanning against. And he actually made a really sharp connection around catching early engineers. I guess that's like a good closing tying those knots there.  
Them: Yeah. I don't know how much early. I guess it just depends on who's where. In terms of, like, I don't think we've had that many engineers on our platform. Overall, like, generally, I don't know.  
Me: Engineers are. Engineers are hard. I think they're absolutely hard. And I think that. I think it. Frankly, it's a shrinking fucking market if we don't have engineers on it, and I think. We can figure out how to get engineers on it more easily than all of the other problems that are solved by going in this direction.  
Them: Yeah, I gotcha.  
Me: I guess is what it always boils down to.  
Them: Yeah. What's the smallest problem? No. Yeah. For him. It sounds like, though, in terms of PMF call. What he identified as his main problem is response rates. Not in sourcing or identifying talent. So it's like, I think. The piece, which I think we could.  
Me: But I do think. I think. Yeah, I do think there's something there. I think that's totally legit, actually. I don't want to glaze over that point. I think that's a genuine consideration. I think. But to your point, maybe we target that with what I was saying around. You get this. Edge on them. Right. You get as much of a what if that was the thing that you got was a highly personalized outreach email that visually, because they all must have. You know how we have a taste for admissions essays.  
Them: Address that.  
Me: Surely they. Surely they have a taste for network outreach emails. And I suspect if we're able to get one that is sufficiently personalized or sort of demonstrate that as an asset, I think that could count.  
Them: I guess it's also, but. At a certain point, I think we're getting, in my mind at least we're getting a little bit ahead of ourselves in terms of what do we have? Like, let's say that we ramp up, and we will be ramping up, like, okay, get the fuck back on Careerspan. Here's a job. For you. Like, what we want to be able to show is an open rate. That's an easier way to do this in terms of this many Careerspan users. Open the fucking email from us saying, here's a job for you, right? That is half the battle. I think there's an extent.  
Me: Oh, I see what you mean. Yeah. Yeah, that does convey engagement.  
Them: That's easy. That's already what we're doing. And we already had really good open rates when Ilsa was sending roles, right? So it's like, I see what you're saying about the personalized outreach message, but at a certain point, it becomes fucking creepy if you have an octave into that. If we're reading their stories and using them against them in some ways. So I would say that from.  
Me: Yeah, yeah, I know. That's a very good point. Very good point. No, totally fair.  
Them: A don't piss off the engineer's perspective, that would piss me off. But if we can show a recruiting agency, even one like Kim, where we're like, yeah, we've got. Not only do we have really good talent, But they're fucking opening. Our emails are not opening LinkedIn messages. That, to me, is just as much of a win, and it's something that we already have the foundation for.  
Me: No. Makes sense. Let's. Sorry. Got hungry.  
Them: Get.  
Me: But yeah, no. Let's do that.  
Them: Co. Ol. Okay. I have been talking out the storm. So I'm going to go chill out, do my Yoga Nidra for 15 minutes and I will be back online.
